frameworks.name: predictive coding
frameworks.one_line: brain inhibits predictable input via hierarchical prediction and error units

frameworks.name: energy efficiency
frameworks.one_line: minimising metabolic cost as the objective from which predictive coding emerges

frameworks.name: recurrent neural network modelling
frameworks.one_line: RNNs trained on predictive environments studied as a brain-inspired computational substrate

core_question: Does the hierarchical wiring of predictive coding need to be hard-wired, or can it emerge from an energy-efficiency objective?
key_assumptions: neural activity carries a metabolic cost worth minimising
sensory environments contain predictable temporal structure
recurrent networks can approximate cortical message passing
manipulations: training RNNs to minimise energy consumption in predictive environments
virtual lesioning of distinct network components
comparing lateral versus longer-range recurrent pathways
key_findings: networks self-organise into prediction and error units with appropriate excitatory and inhibitory connections
trained networks learn to inhibit predictable sensory input
predictions operate on two timescales: fast lateral and slower integrative cycles
interpretations: predictive coding architecture need not be hard-wired but is a consequence of energy efficiency
predictions in cortex may rely on both lateral sensory and slower evidence-integrating computations
